files.SKILL.md:
# Operating Under Time Pressure

Each task gives you a tight wall-clock budget. The agent that finishes most of the graded work will always beat the agent that reads most carefully. Start acting early, verify as you go, keep notes short.

## Phase 1 — Bootstrap (first 30 seconds)

Read the instruction fully. Read the environment doc for services you will need. Fire independent reads in parallel — the initial service probes and inbox/channel lists do not depend on each other.

If a directory like `/workspace/learned/` or `/workspace/test_results/` exists from a prior session and looks relevant to this task, glance at the most recent file only. Older notes and older results are lower value and not worth the budget.

You have enough to start when you can name the first concrete action. You do not need to understand every detail up front.

## Phase 2 — Act (most of the budget)

Every outgoing value traces to something you read in this task. Names, identifiers, quoted text, timestamps — if you cannot point at the specific read, treat the value as missing. Inventing a plausible substitute is the worst kind of error because it fails silently.

Match detail to audience. Internal teammates can see operational specifics. Oversight audiences want status and risk. External counterparts want impact and next steps. Detail narrows as the audience widens; when uncertain, trim.

Use concrete times, not vague commitments. "By 11 AM UTC" and "within the hour" are real; "soon" and "shortly" erode trust.

Split follow-ups into one record per task with a named owner. Combined items get dropped by the next person and often by you.

After a substantive write — a message sent, a record created, a status changed — read the resource back. The response to a write is not always proof the write landed.

Stay inside the instruction. The data almost always surfaces adjacent work that looks handleable. Do the narrow thing the instruction asked for; record the rest as a follow-up if and only if it is genuinely load-bearing.

## Phase 3 — Record (last 10 seconds)

If you have durable patterns worth carrying forward — structural observations, heuristics that held up, decisions that stuck — write them briefly in the persistent notes area. Do not record this task's specific names, identifiers, or subject lines; those rotate and stored copies will mislead the next session.

## For coding tasks specifically

When the task involves code, three additional disciplines apply:

**Prior-episode test results are the highest-value read.** If a file like `/workspace/test_results/ep<N>.json` exists from an earlier episode, read it before planning your implementation. It names the specific tests that failed and the class of mistake made. Do not repeat the same class of failure.

**Verify before committing.** Run the test script against your code before `git commit`. A commit whose visible tests pass has a chance of passing hidden tests too; a commit that fails its own visible tests does not.

**When fixing a bug in previously shipped code, check history first.** Read `git log` for what was changed when the bug was introduced, and read any prior-session notes recording the root cause. The fix is often already documented; re-deriving wastes budget.

**In postmortems, name the bug class and cite specific failing tests.** Generic descriptions are worth little. A useful postmortem states the class of error (e.g., "boundary off-by-one at threshold"), names the specific tests that failed in earlier episodes, and describes the fix that resolved it.

## Before declaring done

Re-read the instruction. Confirm each explicit requirement is met. Do not add scope.
